title: The Eagle

jokes.text: Mike using the presidential code name 'Sparrow' to escort Selina into a restaurant as if it were a security operation
jokes.setup: Mike gives the all-clear into his earpiece like a Secret Service agent securing a location
jokes.punchline: It's a restaurant reservation — 'Table for two under McLintock' — the solemn spy protocol applied to dinner is absurd
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep setup: the machinery of government self-importance applied to something mundane. Works as character comedy because it's so Mike — pompous, clueless about how he looks. The joke lands quietly but establishes tone.

jokes.text: Mike's press briefing deflection: the Meyer Postal Commission reduced post office debt 'from 65 billion to just over 62 billion'
jokes.setup: Reporter asks how the president can focus on the economy while dating; Mike pivots to her fiscal record
jokes.punchline: The 'achievement' is saving 3 billion on a 65 billion debt — barely 5% — delivered as if it's a triumphant accomplishment
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the specific numbers — the writers did the math so you feel the absurdity. Rewatch bonus because the Postal Commission payoff later (the ballot-hoarder fired by Selina's commission) retroactively makes this funnier.

jokes.text: 'Even Wall Street's got a boner for you guys.' / 'Really? The market's up?' / 'No, it's flat. But not down is straight up for us at this point.'
jokes.setup: Ben reports that Wall Street has 'a boner' for Selina's relationship with Baird
jokes.punchline: Selina assumes the market is up; it's flat — and Ben reframes flatness as a success because their baseline is so bad
jokes.explanation: Three-beat joke with excellent economy. The 'boner' setup primes a double entendre, Selina's hopeful misread is a classic straight-man beat, and 'not down is straight up for us' is a killer tag that also communicates genuine political desperation. One of the tighter constructions in the episode.

jokes.text: 'I got a great idea. Why don't we give the Chinese their own log-ins and passwords? Save everybody a lot of time.'
jokes.setup: Aide reports Chinese hackers have breached NSC servers
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'solution' is to just give them official access — fatalistic and absurdist simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Rewatch bonus because this joke gets dramatically ironic when she later falsely blames China for a hack. The 'save everybody a lot of time' tag is economically perfect. Selina's exhausted cynicism is well-served here.

jokes.text: 'Got a few fowl questions.' (about the salmonella chicken farm outbreak)
jokes.setup: Aide informs Selina of a salmonella outbreak at a Missouri chicken farm
jokes.punchline: 'Got a few fowl questions' — foul/fowl pun
jokes.explanation: A groan-worthy pun that lands as a throwaway. Very low character integration — this could be said by anyone. Feels like a writer having fun at the expense of craft. Gets a mild laugh purely from the audacity of saying it in a political briefing.

jokes.text: Charlie says O'Brien won't go down on his wife without biscuits and gravy.
jokes.setup: Selina is checking her phone for sweet messages from Charlie.
jokes.punchline: Instead of reading a romantic DM, she reads aloud an apparently crude political gossip item about the rival candidate's sex life.
jokes.explanation: Extremely Veep — the casual, matter-of-fact delivery of a filthy non-sequitur political rumor. The specificity of 'biscuits and gravy' elevates this from crude to memorably absurd. Reads as something insiders would share as currency.

jokes.text: Selina asking staff to show her which Twitter button to push for nice tweets about her and Charlie, mid-crisis
jokes.setup: The team is discussing the Nevada vote certification crisis
jokes.punchline: Selina interrupts to find ego-stroking tweets about her relationship — total narcissistic non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: Strong character integration — Selina's inability to prioritize anything over personal vanity is her defining flaw, and this moment nails it. The contrast with the gravity of the crisis makes it funnier.

jokes.text: 'I mean, seriously, do we have any reason to think that we're not as fucked as a Senate page here?'
jokes.setup: Selina surveys the vote certification situation
jokes.punchline: The simile 'fucked as a Senate page' — a dark reference to congressional sex scandals, delivered as a casual political metaphor
jokes.explanation: This is sharp Veep writing. The simile is original, the dark political subtext is earned by the show's world, the economy is perfect. 'Senate page' is specific enough to be real and dark enough to be transgressive. Selina's casual use of it as a metric reveals her moral universe. High quotability.

jokes.text: Kent presents statistical evidence of missing ballots: 'Where?' 'I do not know.' 'How many?' 'I do not know.' 'Are you sure?' 'Absolutely.'
jokes.setup: Kent announces he has found 'statistical evidence of missing ballots in Nevada'
jokes.punchline: He has no idea where or how many, but is 'absolutely' sure — certainty of method, total absence of actionable information
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The rhythm is perfect — three rapid-fire question/answer pairs that build to the absurd punchline of 'Absolutely.' The joke is about the gap between Kent's statistical confidence and practical uselessness, which is entirely his character. Rewatch bonus because Kent actually delivers in the end. Economy score near-perfect: six words do the work.

jokes.text: 'Hey, Garfield, we're right in the middle of talking about Nevada, okay?' — Selina calling her aide 'Garfield'
jokes.setup: An aide (Jonah or Gary) interrupts the Nevada crisis meeting with a Vanity Fair photo spread request
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses him with the wrong name — 'Garfield' — communicating contempt through deliberate mis-naming
jokes.explanation: Selina's deliberate wrong-naming of subordinates is a recurring character beat — not a one-off joke. Works as character comedy but isn't especially fresh on its own.

jokes.text: 'What Alan Turing is trying to say is if there are missing votes, he needs time to find them.'
jokes.setup: Kent launches into statistical jargon: 'intercept model,' 'confounding variables'
jokes.punchline: Ben translates it with weary condescension by comparing Kent to Alan Turing — simultaneously insulting Kent's communication skills while complimenting his intellect in the most deflating way
jokes.explanation: Solid joke. Ben's exasperated translator role is well-used here and 'Alan Turing' is a more interesting choice than the obvious 'nerd' insult — it's pointed, specific, and slightly cruel given Turing's fate. Good character integration for both Ben and Kent.

jokes.text: 'The injunction or the dick slapping?' — Amy asking for clarification on what to set up
jokes.setup: Bob says after he's done 'dick slapping' Whitman in the meeting, he'll have agreed to delay certification. Selina says 'Amy, set it up.'
jokes.punchline: Amy deadpans: 'The injunction or the dick slapping?' — treating Bob's crude metaphor as a literal task she might be assigned
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect economy — five words that land enormous impact. Amy's deadpan precision is her defining character trait and this is a textbook deployment of it. Taking the metaphor literally is a classic Veep move but the execution here is exceptional. 'Christ, Amy, the meeting' as the tag is equally precise.

jokes.text: 'I just feel trapped like a Saudi housekeeper.'
jokes.setup: Selina tells Bob she's going crazy in DC and feels trapped
jokes.punchline: The simile 'like a Saudi housekeeper' — a casually dark reference to labor exploitation delivered as a throwaway personal complaint
jokes.explanation: This is very good Veep writing. The simile is shockingly specific, deeply inappropriate for a sitting president, and perfectly captures Selina's moral obliviousness — she's complaining about feeling trapped using an analogy that involves actual human rights abuses she has power to address. The gap between her privilege and her self-pity is enormous and the joke makes you feel it. High originality and character integration.

jokes.text: 'Just let me win the presidency for you and then you can land Air Force One on the Strip and piss in Caesar's fountain if you want.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks Bob if she can get to Nevada during the recount fight
jokes.punchline: Bob's folksy reassurance escalates from 'win the presidency' to 'piss in Caesar's fountain' — the prize is framed as petty and gross
jokes.explanation: Good Bob line. The vulgarity functions as a power fantasy for Selina, and 'piss in Caesar's fountain' is memorable. The escalation from serious (winning the presidency) to petty (fountain desecration) is well-calibrated.

jokes.text: 'Oh, come on, please, Bob, I would never stay at Caesar's.'
jokes.setup: Bob offers to let her piss in Caesar's fountain as a reward
jokes.punchline: Selina's objection is not to the urination but to the hotel's prestige — she's too upscale for Caesar's Palace
jokes.explanation: Exceptional tag. The joke inverts expectations — instead of objecting to the indignity of public urination, Selina objects to the hotel's brand. This is peak Selina: class snobbery trumps basic decorum. Very strong character integration. Works as a callback punchline to #15.

jokes.text: 'See? Crapistan's calling.' — Selina's response to being interrupted by CIA briefing
jokes.setup: Selina is complaining about being trapped; CIA briefing interrupts
jokes.punchline: 'Crapistan's calling' — dismissive geopolitical nickname encapsulating her contempt for the duties of office
jokes.explanation: Good shorthand for Selina's attitude toward foreign policy responsibilities. The invented country name works as a character moment but isn't particularly original — similar jokes have been made in political satire before.

jokes.text: 'Catherine, do not use any of the vulgar parts.' / 'Yeah, but that's like all of it, Mom.' / 'Well, then don't use it.' / 'But it's a doc.' / 'A what?' / 'A documentary.' / 'Then say that.'
jokes.setup: Selina dismisses Catherine's documentary about... presumably all of the above
jokes.punchline: Catherine's 'that's like all of it, Mom' — the documentary would essentially be blank if the vulgar parts are removed; followed by Selina not knowing what a 'doc' is
jokes.explanation: Two jokes stacked: the self-incriminating 'that's like all of it' reveals Selina's conduct, and then 'A what? / A documentary. / Then say that.' nails Selina's contempt for any language she didn't authorize. Strong mother-daughter dynamic.

jokes.text: Dan slept with Amy's sister Sophie thinking she worked for CBS. She works for CVS. 'You sold your dick for bulk iced tea and off-brand cough syrup.'
jokes.setup: Dan is implied to have slept with Sophie to get a CBS connection; colleague reveals Sophie works for CVS, not CBS
jokes.punchline: 'You sold your dick for bulk iced tea and off-brand cough syrup. Don't worry. You're gonna look really cute in a blue vest.'
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and one of the sharpest in Veep's run. The CBS/CVS confusion is simple but the payoff is exceptional: 'bulk iced tea and off-brand cough syrup' is brutally specific, the 'blue vest' callback to CVS uniforms completes the humiliation perfectly, and 'sold your dick' is simultaneously crass and precise. It works on every level — structure, economy, character (Dan's mercenary sexuality), originality. The specificity of the products is what elevates it from good to great.

jokes.text: 'I am not having a good year.' — Dan's reaction beat / talking head after the CVS reveal
jokes.setup: Dan has just been eviscerated for sleeping with the wrong sister at the wrong pharmacy-chain employer
jokes.punchline: The massive understatement — 'I am not having a good year' — delivered to camera as deadpan confession
jokes.explanation: Perfect tag to the CVS bit. Six words doing enormous work. The understatement is massive given what we know about Dan's broader arc. Strong economy score, decent character integration. The camera delivery is what makes it — a rare moment of genuine Dan self-awareness.

jokes.text: 'Do the Israelis know anything about this? Because they're a sneaky bunch of fucks.'
jokes.setup: Intelligence briefing on a failed prisoner identification
jokes.punchline: Selina's casual ethnic characterization of Israeli intelligence, delivered with zero diplomatic filter
jokes.explanation: Functions as character comedy — Selina's foreign policy ignorance and casual bigotry are in character. The joke is mainly that the President of the United States says this. Not especially original within the Veep universe but consistently effective.

jokes.text: Mike using the POTUS reservation system under his own name to score a great table at a hot restaurant: 'When I call, I get an amazing table.'
jokes.setup: Kent asks how Mike secured a reservation at Salt and Moon
jokes.punchline: Mike uses presidential access for personal restaurant perks — the corruption is so petty it loops back to being funny
jokes.explanation: Good Mike joke. The scale of the corruption is perfectly mundane — not bribery, just a nice dinner table. His total unawareness that this is wrong is on-brand.

jokes.text: Mike trying to physically stop Selina from tweeting by calling through the door — while Marjorie blocks him
jokes.setup: Mike realizes Selina is tweeting something damaging about O'Brien and races to stop her
jokes.punchline: He's physically blocked by the Secret Service from getting into the room; can only shout 'If you hear this, do not tweet' through the door
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy beat. Mike's impotence in the face of the Secret Service while a national incident unfolds is classic Veep — the machinery of protection actively enabling disaster. 'If you hear this, do not tweet' is an absurd sentence for the President's press secretary to shout.

jokes.text: Hi, ma'am. If you hear this, do not tweet.
jokes.setup: Mike has apparently gotten close enough to shout into the room.
jokes.punchline: His warning — 'If you hear this, do not tweet' — is simultaneously too late and too absurd, delivered like a hostage negotiator.
jokes.explanation: The conditional phrasing 'if you hear this' is the unexpected grace note — it implies Mike isn't sure he's in earshot, which adds another layer of pathos to his failed intervention.

jokes.text: 'I pushed that feather button.' / 'I know, you tweet — that's a tweet.' — the 'feather button' as Selina's understanding of the tweet icon
jokes.setup: Selina defends herself by saying she pushed 'that feather button'
jokes.punchline: The Twitter bird logo becomes 'the feather button' — her total disconnect from digital literacy while running the country
jokes.explanation: Sharp specific detail that elevates the broader 'president is bad at tech' joke. 'Feather button' is exactly the kind of wrong-but-logical description that a technologically illiterate person in their 50s would use. High character integration.

jokes.text: 'Well, what the FOL are we supposed to do about this?'
jokes.setup: The Swedish PM has retweeted Selina's tweet with 'LOL'; Selina is furious
jokes.punchline: 'FOL' — Selina's version of 'LOL' applied to her own exasperated question, probably meaning 'Fuck Out Loud' — mixing political outrage with internet slang she barely understands
jokes.explanation: Excellent. Three letters doing a lot of work. It's Selina accidentally inventing a new acronym while trying to use one she only half understands — the joke is that she's trying to be digitally fluent and isn't. Very memorable, very quotable.

jokes.text: 'Phones are hacked all the time to steal pictures of celebrities' penises and whatnot.'
jokes.setup: Kent suggests they disavow the tweets by blaming a hack
jokes.punchline: Kent's robotic delivery of 'celebrities' penises and whatnot' — the 'whatnot' doing enormous work as Kent's euphemistic catch-all for nude photo scandals
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely in the word 'whatnot' — Kent's clinical detachment from tabloid culture expressed through the most sterile possible vocabulary. 'Celebrities' penises and whatnot' is a genuinely funny sentence because of what 'whatnot' implies. Perfect character integration for Kent's voice.

jokes.text: 'So is that what I think happened? That China tweeted this?' / 'Oh, no, ma'am, no. You have no idea what happened.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks for confirmation of the fabricated narrative
jokes.punchline: Mike's 'You have no idea what happened' — technically advising plausible deniability but functionally insulting her competence
jokes.explanation: Efficient and sharp. The dual meaning of 'you have no idea what happened' (legal advice + description of reality) is well-deployed. Mike is simultaneously coaching her and being accidentally accurate.

jokes.text: 'No, you don't, Mike. History has proven that.' — Selina shutting Mike down when he claims to have a better idea
jokes.setup: Mike says 'Actually, ma'am, I have a better idea'
jokes.punchline: 'No, you don't, Mike. History has proven that.' — absolute, delivered with total conviction
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. 'History has proven that' is a perfect construction — it's grandiose, devastating, and economical. The joke is that Selina invokes history as if Mike's bad ideas are a documented pattern of record. Economy near-perfect: six words, complete character assassination. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Why don't you just tell the truth? / I don't sound like that. / That's exactly how you sound. / No, I don't. / Spot-on. / Yeah, it's pretty close.
jokes.setup: Someone impersonates Selina suggesting she 'tell the truth,' and she objects to the impression.
jokes.punchline: The room confirms the impression is accurate — multiple people validating it while Selina insists it's wrong, revealing her lack of self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: The comedy of multiple subordinates all confirming the impression while Selina insists it's wrong is a nice ensemble beat. 'Yeah, it's pretty close' as the final grudging concession is the right note.

jokes.text: 'Are we paying you by the word here, Kent, or what?'
jokes.setup: Kent explains his statistical methodology at length: 'district-by-district regression analysis'
jokes.punchline: Selina's frustration expressed as a billing joke — treating Kent's verbosity as a professional vice
jokes.explanation: Good Selina/Kent dynamic joke. The billing metaphor is not especially original but it's sharp and well-placed. Selina's contempt for jargon is consistent character work.

jokes.text: 'I will not dignify the president's petty remarks with a response.' — O'Brien, and then he responds at length
jokes.setup: O'Brien announces he won't respond to Selina's tweet about him
jokes.punchline: He then immediately responds at length, including diabetes statistics
jokes.explanation: Classic political irony — the 'I won't dignify this with a response' response. Not original as a joke form, but the diabetes non-sequitur ('And yet he's responding') adds a functional tag. Selina's 'And yet he's responding' is the actual punchline.

jokes.text: 'And yet he's responding.'
jokes.setup: O'Brien says he won't dignify Selina's remarks with a response
jokes.punchline: Selina states the obvious fact that he is, in fact, responding — delivered as a dry observation
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum economy. The observation is true, obvious, and perfectly timed. Not especially original but executed with precision.

jokes.text: O'Brien pivots from 'I won't respond' to a speech about 29 million diabetes sufferers
jokes.setup: O'Brien says he won't respond to the tweet, then pivots to policy
jokes.punchline: Diabetes statistics as a rhetorical counter-move to being called out by the president — the non-sequitur is the joke
jokes.explanation: Mildly funny absurdist moment. The joke is that politicians flee to unrelated statistics when cornered, and diabetes is a mundane enough topic to make it funny. 'Oh, who cares?' is Selina's perfect reaction tag.

jokes.text: 'Nev-ADD-a' — someone corrects Selina's pronunciation of Nevada mid-crisis
jokes.setup: Selina says 'Ne-VAH-da' in the middle of a serious discussion about winning the state
jokes.punchline: A staffer quietly corrects her pronunciation — the pedantry is absurd given the stakes; Selina's mispronunciation of the pivotal state is character-perfect
jokes.explanation: Very sharp. The joke is precise and specific — she's fighting for this state's votes and doesn't even know how to say its name. The timing (mid-sentence in a crisis) is perfect. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'You're the one who suggested we all live in the Matrix.' / 'Ma'am, nobody chose to live in the Matrix. The machines rose up and placed humans in the Matrix so they could use them as a biological power source.'
jokes.setup: Selina tells Ben 'In reality? I know reality. You're the one who suggested we all live in the Matrix.'
jokes.punchline: Ben pedantically corrects the Matrix mythology — 'nobody chose to live in the Matrix' — instead of engaging with the political argument
jokes.explanation: Wonderful absurdist detour. Ben's correction is completely correct and completely beside the point, and the detail ('biological power source') is funny because of how specific it is. The exchange ends on 'Whose side were you on?' which is perfect — Selina taking the Matrix as a literal alignment question. One of the better absurdist moments in the episode.

jokes.text: 'Whose side were you on?' — Selina responding to Ben's Matrix explanation
jokes.setup: Ben has just explained that humans didn't choose to live in the Matrix
jokes.punchline: Selina asks which side Ben was on — treating the Matrix as a real geopolitical conflict she should have been briefed on
jokes.explanation: Exceptional tag. Four words that reveal Selina's total confusion while also being genuinely funny as a standalone line. The joke is that 'Whose side were you on?' is exactly how a politician frames every conflict — and she's applying it to a 1999 sci-fi film. Perfect economy, high character integration.

jokes.text: 'Or we could go to the United Nations and have them issue...' / 'Have them what? Have them help us park in front of a fire hydrant? I mean, come on.'
jokes.setup: Someone suggests going to the UN to respond to China
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses the UN with the fire hydrant parking joke — implying the UN is useless for anything meaningful
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire. The parking/fire hydrant metaphor for UN authority is specific, unexpected, and very funny — it's a New York-specific reference (UN is in NYC, no parking near fire hydrants) that works as a genuine diplomatic critique. Good economy, high quotability.

jokes.text: Have them what? Have them help us park in front of a fire hydrant? I mean, come on.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests going to the UN for help.
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses the UN as only useful for parking enforcement exemptions — a reference to the real diplomatic practice of UN officials ignoring parking tickets in NYC.
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire with real-world grounding. The UN/parking ticket joke is based on a known real phenomenon (diplomatic immunity for parking) and works as a sophisticated dismissal of the organization's efficacy. Selina's contempt is well-channeled.

jokes.text: Jesus, you're still here? / I don't know why. My only jobs seem to be asking Bob and finding out what Bob thinks.
jokes.setup: Amy is lingering with nothing to do since Bob has taken over her functions.
jokes.punchline: She reduces her entire job description to 'asking Bob' and 'finding out what Bob thinks' — perfectly capturing how she's been sidelined.
jokes.explanation: Good Amy joke. The reductive self-summation of her job is funny and grounds the episode's subplot about Bob displacing her. Character-specific and efficient.

jokes.text: 'Oh, but on your way, would you mind stopping at ABC News and picking up some Advil? Oh, did I say ABC News? I meant Rite Aid.'
jokes.setup: Amy declines Dan's drink invitation; he sends her on her way
jokes.punchline: Dan deliberately 'mixes up' Rite Aid and ABC News — a callback to Amy's sister working at CVS — to remind Amy he slept with her sister at a pharmacy
jokes.explanation: Good callback construction. Dan is rubbing it in by using a pharmacy name, and the 'correction' from ABC News to Rite Aid mirrors the CBS/CVS reveal. Not as strong as the original joke but functional as a sustained dig.

jokes.text: Oh, but on your way, would you mind stopping at ABC News and picking up some Advil? Oh, did I say ABC News? I meant Rite Aid.
jokes.setup: Amy has just told Dan she won't have a drink with him.
jokes.punchline: Dan fake-mixes up ABC News and Rite Aid — a callback dig at Amy about her previous career humiliation (the CVS joke inverted), implying she now belongs at a pharmacy chain.
jokes.explanation: Functional come-back. Dan weaponizing Amy's marginalization — comparing her position to a Rite Aid — is sharp, though not quite as devastating as the CBS/CVS original.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I think you're forgetting something, Amy, is that I still had sex with your sister, so...' / 'Good night, have a pleasant evening, and I had sex with your sister.'
jokes.setup: Amy appears to be ending the conversation and winning the exchange
jokes.punchline: Dan returns to the one card he has — 'I had sex with your sister' — as if this is a rhetorical trump card in a professional dispute
jokes.explanation: Strong running gag on the sister beat. The comedy is that Dan is weaponizing the revelation as a power move even though it reflects worse on him. 'Good night, have a pleasant evening, and I had sex with your sister' is a great line — the formal 'good night' setup makes the crude ending land harder.

jokes.text: 'You might actually want to go to Rite Aid. Get some Valtrex.'
jokes.setup: Dan has just bragged again about sleeping with Amy's sister
jokes.punchline: Amy suggests he get Valtrex (herpes medication) from Rite Aid — implying the sister gave him an STI
jokes.explanation: Perfect closing line for this sequence. It ties together the pharmacy running gag (Rite Aid), the sister storyline, and gives Amy the final kill shot. 'Valtrex' is specific enough (herpes medication, not just 'STI pills') to maximize the insult. Amy wins the exchange definitively. Strong structure and economy, high impact.

jokes.text: The McLintock party has already been seated. / No, no, that's impossible 'cause he's Mike McLintock. / Show her your driver's license. / I can't find it. / Again?
jokes.setup: Mike arrives at his own reservation but is told his party has already been seated — someone has stolen his table.
jokes.punchline: When Mike is challenged to prove his identity, he can't find his driver's license — which has apparently happened before ('Again?').
jokes.explanation: Solid Mike humiliation setup. The payoff — 'Again?' — implies this is a pattern of incompetence, which is perfectly on-brand.

jokes.text: The restaurant confrontation: 'This is my fat johnson and you can suck it as long as you promise not to put your dirty gay mustache on it. What is that, some kind of side effect from your AIDS medication or did you shave it off of Freddie Mercury's corpse?'
jokes.setup: Furlong's table has been taken over by Mike using the POTUS reservation scam; Furlong is enraged
jokes.punchline: The full Furlong verbal assault on Mike — escalating from the table dispute to a sequence of aggressively offensive insults about Will's wife's appearance and Mike's character
jokes.explanation: Furlong's insult comedy is a specific comedic register — transgressive shock value over craft. The jokes are deliberately offensive but not especially well-constructed beyond the shock. The AIDS medication/Freddie Mercury line is the most creative element. Scored lower on earned_vs_cheap because this style leans on taboo rather than wit, though it's very much Furlong's character.

jokes.text: 'Jesus, you think I married that?' / 'That's Will's wife.' — Furlong on seeing Will's wife at the table
jokes.setup: Mrs. Furlong is sitting next to the woman Furlong has been insulting
jokes.punchline: 'Jesus, you think I married that?' — Furlong denies being married to the woman he just insulted, then learns it's Will's wife; pivots to insulting her as such
jokes.explanation: The reveal that it's Will's wife functions as a mild misdirection — the insult was aimed at the wrong person. Furlong's complete lack of remorse is on-character. 'I guess Will likes them old' as an additional insult is economical but not especially creative.

jokes.text: 'Christ, she looks like a Batman villain.'
jokes.setup: Furlong watches as Mrs. Furlong (his actual wife) approaches
jokes.punchline: He describes his own wife as looking like a Batman villain — with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: Good Furlong line. The specificity of 'Batman villain' is better than a generic insult, and the fact that it's his own wife is the crucial detail. Economy is good.

jokes.text: 'Does a bear piss in the shower?'
jokes.setup: Selina asks Bob if he thinks her instincts are right; he responds with a folksy affirmation
jokes.punchline: 'Does a bear piss in the shower?' — a mangled 'does a bear shit in the woods' idiom, replacing the familiar phrase with something weirder and more specific
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The joke works on two levels: on first watch it's a funny Bob-ism; on rewatch it's a sign of his cognitive decline — he's scrambling the familiar idiom, which is exactly what someone with Alzheimer's does. 'Shower' is funnier than 'woods' because it's wrong in a specific, imaginable way. High originality, excellent economy, high quotability.

jokes.text: 'You caught the fish, now gut the fish.' / 'You're wearing the fireman's hat, now put out the fire.' / 'Yes! This is better than phone sex.' / 'Well, I just came.'
jokes.setup: Bob is giving Selina folksy leadership metaphors to encourage her to be bold on China
jokes.punchline: The escalating absurdity of the metaphors leads Selina to say 'This is better than phone sex' — and Bob completes the joke with 'Well, I just came'
jokes.explanation: Excellent sequence. The escalation of Bob's folksy idioms ('caught the fish / gut the fish,' 'fireman's hat / put out the fire') builds to Selina breaking the fourth wall of her own metaphor to call it phone sex — and Bob's 'Well, I just came' is a perfect button. Rewatch bonus because knowing Bob's condition, his sexual non sequitur takes on a darker tinge. High impact.

jokes.text: You caught the fish, now gut the fish. / You're wearing the fireman's hat, now put out the fire. / Yes! This is better than phone sex. / Well, I just came.
jokes.setup: Bob is motivating Selina with folksy metaphors.
jokes.punchline: The accumulation of metaphors ('caught the fish/gut the fish,' 'fireman's hat/put out the fire') escalates to Selina's 'this is better than phone sex' and Bob's 'well, I just came' — a grotesque but perfectly timed admission.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's peak moments. The folksy metaphor escalation builds perfectly, Selina's 'phone sex' line is unexpected but earned, and Bob's 'well, I just came' is the single greatest possible button — maximally crude, delivered presumably with complete serenity. The structural arc from motivational speech to mutual gratification is textbook Veep escalation comedy at its best.

jokes.text: 'There's a new stegosaurus.' / 'That's not my problem.'
jokes.setup: They arrive at the Museum of Natural History; Gary enthusiastically notes a new stegosaurus display
jokes.punchline: Selina's complete indifference — 'That's not my problem' — dismissing a dinosaur as if it were a policy complaint
jokes.explanation: Sharp. 'That's not my problem' is a perfect presidential deflection applied to something completely harmless. Gary's delight vs. Selina's contempt is a classic dynamic, and 'absolutely, ma'am' from the staffer who agrees the stegosaurus is not her problem is a good background beat.

jokes.text: There's a new stegosaurus. / That's not my problem.
jokes.setup: Selina arrives at the Museum of Natural History for the charity event; Gary mentions something about a stegosaurus.
jokes.punchline: The existential brevity of Selina's 'That's not my problem' in response to a new dinosaur exhibit — while she's fighting to become president — is a perfect comedy of presidential priority.
jokes.explanation: Deceptively simple and very good. The conversation snippet 'There's a new stegosaurus / That's not my problem' works perfectly because it drops us in mid-thought. The abrupt dismissal of a dinosaur discovery as beyond Selina's concerns is wonderfully absurd and character-perfect.

jokes.text: God, please back up your face, Amy, okay? Back it up.
jokes.setup: Amy tries to raise concerns about Bob to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to an unwanted conversation is to tell Amy to physically remove her face from proximity — treating Amy's concern as a spatial violation.
jokes.explanation: 'Back up your face' is an extraordinary Selina-ism — treating someone's physical presence as the problem, not their words. 'Back it up' as the follow-up insistence makes it a command. Very quotable, highly character-specific.

jokes.text: Selina reassuring Amy that she's 'very important to the campaign' and doing 'a great, very good job' — clearly to manage her suspicions about Bob
jokes.setup: Amy tries to raise concerns about Bob; Selina interrupts with over-the-top false reassurance
jokes.punchline: 'A great, great, very good job.' / 'One heck of a job, Amy.' — the transparent flattery makes it obvious Selina is managing Amy rather than listening
jokes.explanation: Good political deflection comedy. The 'great, great, very good job' downgrade from 'great' to 'very good' within a single sentence is a subtle but funny detail. Ben's rote 'One heck of a job' is a nice echo of the Bush/Katrina 'Brownie, you're doing a heck of a job' moment.

jokes.text: 'Those things cause cancer, you know.' — Bob's casual cell phone comment
jokes.setup: Someone asks if everyone's cell phones are off before the meeting
jokes.punchline: Bob's aside: 'Those things cause cancer, you know. That's what I hear.' — casual medical misinformation delivered with folksy confidence
jokes.explanation: Rewatch bonus for the same reason as other Bob moments — on rewatch you're watching for signs of decline. On first watch it reads as folksy Bob; on rewatch it's another data point. The joke itself is mild — cell-phone cancer fears are well-worn comedic territory.

jokes.text: 'I still got the cabin.' — Bob's response to Whitman mentioning his wife at Bob's ski cabin
jokes.setup: Whitman says the last time they met was at Bob's ski cabin with his wife — implying Bob's wife is dead or gone
jokes.punchline: 'I still got the cabin.' — Bob's response is either a non-sequitur that ignores the implicit condolence, or a confused deflection; the pause before it is the joke
jokes.explanation: Quietly exceptional. The joke is in what isn't said — the absence of a normal response to what is clearly an implied condolence creates an enormous awkward silence, and Bob's '...I still got the cabin' is either the most deflating response imaginable or a sign his memory is going. Economy is near-perfect: five words do the work of a monologue. Rewatch bonus because it's one of the clearest cognitive clues.

jokes.text: Look, Bob. Bob. We all know you're as folksy as a butter churn lamp.
jokes.setup: Bob is opening with his apple pie folk metaphor; Ben cuts through it.
jokes.punchline: 'Folksy as a butter churn lamp' — Ben acknowledges Bob's down-home style with a hyperspecific rustic object that actually implies something outdated and decorative, not useful.
jokes.explanation: Excellent line. 'Butter churn lamp' is a genuinely original coinage — it's so specific it feels found rather than invented. The insult works on two levels: it's a compliment ('folksy') that also implies Bob is decorative/antique/useless. Could comfortably sit next to an 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Bob's folksy 'thumb in the apple pie' speech at the Whitman meeting, which Whitman immediately cuts off: 'We all know you're as folksy as a butter churn lamp, but let me cut to the chase.'
jokes.setup: Bob opens the meeting with 'What we have here is a classic thumb in the apple pie situation and Grandma is mad as hell'
jokes.punchline: Whitman shuts it down with 'as folksy as a butter churn lamp' — a great specific simile that both mocks and dismisses Bob's entire rhetorical style
jokes.explanation: 'Butter churn lamp' is a very specific and funny object — both rustic (butter churn) and domesticated (lamp), implying Bob's folksiness is decorative rather than functional. Whitman proves to be a capable comic foil. Good line.

jokes.text: Just all of this just screams Grace Kelly. / I know. / I don't like my eyes, though. / What? / Yeah, I feel like I've got two diaper bags underneath. / No, no, no, no. I don't think... / Oh, God. / I need my ring.
jokes.setup: Selina is getting dressed/ready and enjoying the flattery; then she abruptly starts criticizing her appearance.
jokes.punchline: The whiplash from 'Grace Kelly' to 'two diaper bags underneath my eyes' is a perfect Selina self-image joke — the grandiosity and the insecurity coexist in rapid succession.
jokes.explanation: 'Two diaper bags underneath' is a great specific image for under-eye bags. The rapid oscillation between Selina's narcissism and insecurity is character-rich.

jokes.text: 'There's never been a Whitman's Sampler I couldn't take apart piece by piece and shit out, Madam President.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks Bob about the Whitman meeting; he reassures her
jokes.punchline: The Whitman/Whitman's Sampler pun escalated to its most scatological possible conclusion
jokes.explanation: The wordplay on Whitman/Whitman's Sampler is clean, and 'shit out' is the perfectly gross button. Rewatch bonus: this is Bob's most confident moment and knowing what comes next (his cognitive collapse) makes it poignant-funny.

jokes.text: 'I love to hear that. Okay, you're hanging on to me like a skin tag.'
jokes.setup: Bob is clinging to Selina as she tries to prepare for her appearance
jokes.punchline: 'You're hanging on to me like a skin tag' — a body horror simile that's both vivid and specific
jokes.explanation: Strong Selina simile. 'Skin tag' is specific, vivid, and slightly disgusting — which is exactly how Selina sees unwanted human contact. The image is memorable and character-specific.

jokes.text: 'A lady president? Yeah, maybe in the year 2000.' — Bob, in the Whitman meeting, clearly confused
jokes.setup: Bob has wandered into the Whitman meeting in a confused state
jokes.punchline: He says 'A lady president? Yeah, maybe in the year 2000' — not only confused about who Selina is, but revealing his retrograde politics while cognitively compromised
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. 'A lady president? Yeah, maybe in the year 2000' is doing two things: revealing Bob's Alzheimer's (he doesn't know who the president is or what year it is) and revealing the sexism that his folksy competence had previously masked. The comedy is uncomfortable, which is exactly where Veep operates best. 'Year 2000' is funnier than any other year because it's both retrograde and specific. High marks across the board.

jokes.text: 'You sure she just texted or did she tweet the entire world?'
jokes.setup: Gary tells Baird that Selina just texted and is running late
jokes.punchline: Baird's knowing reference to the Twitter scandal — the new boyfriend already knows about the tweet incident
jokes.explanation: Good callback. Baird using the tweet scandal as a knowing joke shows he's already been inducted into the chaos of dating Selina. 'Tweet the entire world' is a nice specific formulation.

jokes.text: It's 250 grand. I bought the table. Sit down.
jokes.setup: Gary is uncertain whether he should sit at Charlie's charity dinner table.
jokes.punchline: Charlie's casually stated '250 grand / I bought the table / sit down' reveals the absurd scale of charity fundraising — and reduces Gary's awkwardness to a math problem.
jokes.explanation: Good economy. Three short statements that establish wealthy nonchalance. The comedy is in the casual drop of '250 grand' as a social solution.

jokes.text: Gary explaining he's 'a key member of the Meyer administration' to get a seat at Baird's dinner table
jokes.setup: Baird seats Gary at the fancy charity dinner, telling him it's fine
jokes.punchline: Gary describes himself as 'a key member of the Meyer administration' — his grandiose self-description for what is essentially being Selina's personal bag carrier
jokes.explanation: Good Gary character moment. The gap between his self-assessment ('key member') and reality (bag carrier, no business card — 'just a bag') is the joke. 'No, just a bag' is the quiet killer — confirming his actual role literally.

jokes.text: He just seemed like the same old Bob to me. / Ma'am, this morning he told me the Jews cause hurricanes.
jokes.setup: The team is debating how mentally impaired Bob is; Gary defends him as 'the same old Bob.'
jokes.punchline: Jonah drops the specific detail — 'he told me the Jews cause hurricanes' — as definitive evidence of Bob's decline.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single most quoted line. 'The Jews cause hurricanes' is perfectly calibrated: specific enough to be absurd rather than generic, dark enough to be shocking, and immediately diagnostic of advanced cognitive decline. Delivered in the context of whether Bob is 'really that bad,' it definitively settles the debate. The specificity (why hurricanes? why Jews?) makes it funnier than any vaguer conspiracy theory. This is Veep at its sharpest.

jokes.text: 'You have a card?' / 'No, just a bag.' — Gary at the charity dinner
jokes.setup: Dinner guest asks Gary for his card as a 'key member of the administration'
jokes.punchline: 'No, just a bag.' — Gary's card is a bag. He literally has no business card; his professional identity is a bag.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy — three words perfectly encapsulate Gary's entire character and position. 'No, just a bag' is the funniest possible answer to 'you have a card?' because a bag is both his literal answer (he's holding a bag) and the perfect metaphor for his role. The joke is built into Gary's existence. Perfect character integration. Economy score 10 — maximum funny per word.

jokes.text: 'Ned Mitchell died in 2006. And the 'Las Vegas Gazette' stopped publishing in 2007.'
jokes.setup: Selina instructed her team to get her on the phone with Ned Mitchell from the 'Las Vegas Gazette' — a contact that Bob had recommended
jokes.punchline: Jonah's revelation: the contact has been dead for years, and the paper no longer exists — Bob's information is over a decade out of date
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke with perfect structure. The double reveal (man is dead; paper is defunct) delivered by Jonah — who is usually the butt of jokes — lands as a genuine bombshell. Rewatch bonus because it's the clearest evidence of Bob's Alzheimer's and pays off the earlier planted hints. The specificity of years (2006, 2007) makes it funnier and more damning.

jokes.text: Ma'am, at his age, if he can take a shit whenever he wants, that's a major victory.
jokes.setup: Jonah panics about Bob potentially defecating in the meeting.
jokes.punchline: Ben's geriatric spin — framing voluntary bowel control as a 'major victory' for an older man — is darkly funny and completely deflates the panic.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ben line. The deadpan reframe of the panic into an affirmative observation about geriatric independence is unexpected and very funny. 'Major victory' does the work — the clinical understatement of a man who has been around power long enough to have very low expectations.

jokes.text: 'I was able to get in touch with Susan Bailey at the 'Reno Star,' which is not a newspaper, it's a legal brothel. But she did seem excited to help the campaign in whichever way she could.'
jokes.setup: Jonah followed up on the other contact Bob recommended: Susan Bailey at the 'Reno Star'
jokes.punchline: The 'Reno Star' is a legal brothel; Susan Bailey is a sex worker willing to help the campaign
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode. The escalation from 'dead journalist' to 'legal brothel' is perfectly structured — each Bob contact revelation is worse than the last. 'Not a newspaper, it's a legal brothel' is a great construction, and 'she did seem excited to help the campaign in whichever way she could' is an absolutely perfect tag — delivered with Jonah's total deadpan obliviousness. This joke reveals Bob's dementia, skewers Nevada's legal brothel industry, and has a killer tag line. Rewatch bonus: you notice Jonah didn't say he didn't find this out personally.

jokes.text: 'Ma'am, this morning he told me the Jews cause hurricanes.'
jokes.setup: People are defending Bob or suggesting he seemed fine
jokes.punchline: Ben's revelation: Bob told him this morning that 'the Jews cause hurricanes' — his antisemitic delusion is the clearest evidence yet of breakdown
jokes.explanation: Brutally funny line that would not work in any other register. The specificity — not just 'confused' but with a paranoid, specific conspiracy theory — is what makes it funny rather than just sad. 'The Jews cause hurricanes' as an Alzheimer's symptom is darkly specific. Ben's delivery as a straightforward data point ('this morning he told me') is perfect. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: 'All right, Amy, this meeting cannot make it past the School Book Depository. I'm loading my gun and heading to the sixth floor.'
jokes.setup: Selina tells Amy to stop the Whitman meeting from proceeding past a certain point
jokes.punchline: Amy responds with a JFK assassination metaphor — 'School Book Depository,' 'loading my gun,' 'sixth floor' — for stopping a political meeting
jokes.explanation: Very good Veep joke. Amy's total commitment to the assassination metaphor is perfectly in character — she's all business, always uses the most aggressive possible framing. The specificity (School Book Depository, sixth floor — not just 'sniper') shows the writers went all the way with it. Dark subversion at its best.

jokes.text: 'Well, don't worry, ma'am. We'll get him on the first flight out of Nevada and we'll shove his ass out somewhere over Oklahoma.'
jokes.setup: Selina panics that Bob knows the China hack was fabricated; Ben tries to reassure her
jokes.punchline: Ben's solution is to eject Bob from a plane over Oklahoma — casual brutality as political problem-solving
jokes.explanation: Dark and competent. 'Shove his ass out somewhere over Oklahoma' is a good specific image — Oklahoma being chosen as the most forgettable, inconsequential location is a nice regional dig. The casual brutality toward a man we've been watching decline is perfectly Ben.

jokes.text: 'Alzheimer's or no, I image-searched Susan Bailey and she is legit fuckable.'
jokes.setup: The team is discussing Bob's Alzheimer's and how to handle Susan Bailey the brothel owner
jokes.punchline: Jonah's priorities are revealed: regardless of the political crisis or Bob's condition, he looked up the brothel owner and found her attractive
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jonah joke. 'Alzheimer's or no' is the key phrase — he's acknowledging the pathos of the situation and immediately pivoting to the only thing he cares about. 'Legit fuckable' as a clinical assessment is peak Jonah register. Character integration is near-perfect — this is exactly and only Jonah.

jokes.text: Alzheimer's or no, I image-searched Susan Bailey and she is legit fuckable.
jokes.setup: The team has discovered Bob's confusion about Susan Bailey being a legal brothel rather than a journalist.
jokes.punchline: Jonah, after a moment of apparent reflection on Bob's deterioration, makes clear his takeaway is that the brothel worker is attractive.
jokes.explanation: Peak Jonah — the 'Alzheimer's or no' preamble suggests he's about to make a sympathetic observation about Bob, then the complete pivot reveals his actual concern. The digression into the brothel worker's attractiveness is perfectly Jonah in its obliviousness and vulgarity.

jokes.text: 'Honestly, her rates are reasonable.'
jokes.setup: Jonah has been cut off / told to shut up; continues the Susan Bailey thread anyway
jokes.punchline: He's researched the brothel's pricing — and reports it's reasonable — while Dan's voicemail plays in the background
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum Jonah. 'Honestly, her rates are reasonable' implies he compared rates, considered it carefully, and is now reporting to his colleagues as a professional courtesy. The word 'honestly' — as if this is earnest financial advice — is the key to the joke. Near-perfect economy, maximum character integration.

jokes.text: 'Amy, if you had rented me a Sebring, yes.' — Richard on whether he can drive faster to the Whitman meeting
jokes.setup: Amy asks if Richard can drive any faster to intercept Bob at the Whitman meeting
jokes.punchline: Richard blames the car — specifically a Chrysler Sebring — as the limiting factor, as if a different rental car would solve the crisis
jokes.explanation: Classic Richard. The specificity of 'a Sebring' — notoriously the most mediocre of rental cars — is what makes the joke work. Richard has opinions about rental cars in a crisis. High character integration.

jokes.text: 'This is like that famous scene from the movie 'Heat.' You know, with De Niro and Brenneman. Just thinking about that scene gives me goose bumps.'
jokes.setup: Richard and Amy are racing to stop Bob at the Whitman meeting
jokes.punchline: Richard compares their mission to a scene from Heat — but names Amy Brenneman instead of Al Pacino as De Niro's co-star; the wrong half of his movie knowledge is confident
jokes.explanation: Excellent Richard joke. The joke requires knowledge that Amy Brenneman is in Heat (as De Niro's wife) but is not the famous scene partner — it's Pacino. Richard has seen the movie, been moved by it, remembered the wrong person, and is completely confident. 'Just thinking about that scene gives me goose bumps' is a great tag — he's getting emotional about the wrong Heat scene. Very specific, very Richard.

jokes.text: Honestly, I was gonna ask you that.
jokes.setup: Whitman asks Richard where Bob is.
jokes.punchline: Richard has absolutely no idea where his own principal is and is surprised to be asked, as if the question was equally mysterious to him.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Richard deadpan. The joke is his complete inability to pretend he knows what's going on — he responds to a question about his own colleague with genuine mutual curiosity. Character-perfect economy.

jokes.text: Charlie Baird's speech: 'my date is running late. Presidents, right? Well, hopefully she arrives before we all turn into fossils.'
jokes.setup: Baird is hosting a museum gala; Selina is late
jokes.punchline: 'Before we all turn into fossils' — museum/dinosaur joke, appropriate to the Natural History Museum setting; also a mild dig at the age of the crowd
jokes.explanation: Competent gala joke, appropriate to setting. The fossil/museum connection is obvious but well-delivered. 'Presidents, right?' is a nice casual moment of Baird being charming-but-resigned to dating Selina.

jokes.text: Selina frantically calls staff who don't answer; Ben tries Dan again; they discover Bob left the meeting mid-session and got in a cab
jokes.setup: Selina needs to know what's happening in the Whitman meeting; nobody answers; finally Dan picks up
jokes.punchline: The Eagle has 'flown out onto the street and gotten in a cab' — Bob Bradley, the legendary political operative, left an important meeting to hail a taxi
jokes.explanation: Good escalation beat. The code name 'Eagle flying out' applied to an old man wandering into traffic for a cab is a good comedic image. The gap between his code name and his current state is poignant and funny.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, turns out he's nuttier than a squirrel's diaper.'
jokes.setup: The team discusses that Bob left the Whitman meeting
jokes.punchline: 'Nuttier than a squirrel's diaper' — a simile that invents an object (squirrel's diaper) to intensify the 'nuts' idiom
jokes.explanation: Very good Jonah line. 'Squirrel's diaper' is an invented object that earns its laugh — squirrels don't wear diapers, but if they did, they'd be very full of nuts. The illogic is internally consistent. Highly quotable — a vivid, original image delivered with Jonah's confidence.

jokes.text: 'Shut the fuck up. Shut the fuck up, Jonah.' — Selina, then Dan, both immediately saying the same thing
jokes.setup: Jonah makes his comment about Bob's sanity during a tense phone call
jokes.punchline: Selina says 'Tell Jonah to shut the fuck up' and Dan immediately says 'Shut the fuck up' — the real-time translation is instant and comes twice
jokes.explanation: Functional running joke in the episode — Jonah being told to shut up is reliable if not especially inventive. The double-shot (Selina then Dan) works rhythmically.

jokes.text: Richard left alone with Whitman: 'Do you mind if I get a picture with you for my blog 'Let's Talk About Splett'?'
jokes.setup: Richard is alone with the opposing counsel Whitman after Bob walked out
jokes.punchline: Rather than negotiating, he asks for a selfie for his personal blog, which is called 'Let's Talk About Splett'
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The blog name 'Let's Talk About Splett' is a perfect invention — self-promotional, weirdly enthusiastic, completely at odds with the political crisis happening. That he would pivot from saving the presidency to asking for a blog photo in a moment of maximum stakes is peak Richard. The name 'Splett' being his own last name makes it even more Richard. Near-perfect character integration. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'Holy bacon double Asperger's.'
jokes.setup: Richard reveals he's learned from Whitman that the ballots are in Washoe County
jokes.punchline: Jonah's exclamation is a mangled 'holy bacon double cheeseburger' crossed with Asperger's — possibly a (very Jonah) inadvertent reference to Kent's statistical autism-coded character, possibly just random
jokes.explanation: A pure Jonah exclamation — transgressive, confused, specific in the wrong ways. The joke is that this is how Jonah expresses amazement. 'Asperger's' in place of the expected food item is aggressively wrong in a characteristically Jonah way. Not structurally sophisticated but highly quotable.

jokes.text: Amy, what are your top five favorite De Niro movies? And you can't say 'Meet the Parents' 'cause that's automatically number one.
jokes.setup: The team is in a crisis moment — Richard has just accidentally gotten Whitman to reveal the ballots' location.
jokes.punchline: Richard immediately pivots to a completely irrelevant De Niro movie discussion, treating the crisis as resolved and celebrating through pop culture chat.
jokes.explanation: Richard at his best — the post-crisis De Niro list is delivered with the earnestness of a man who genuinely wants to have this conversation. 'Meet the Parents' as his automatic #1 is a perfect character detail: it's the crowd-pleasing choice, not the cinephile's choice.

jokes.text: 'Amy, what are your top five favorite De Niro movies? And you can't say 'Meet the Parents' 'cause that's automatically number one.'
jokes.setup: Amy is trying to thank Richard for his work at the Whitman meeting; Richard ignores the context
jokes.punchline: Richard's immediate pivot to a De Niro top-five request — mid-crisis celebration — while pre-emptively disqualifying Meet the Parents as 'automatically number one'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Richard joke and a great callback to the Heat confusion earlier. Richard's De Niro enthusiasm is now established, and his certainty that Meet the Parents is number one (not Goodfellas, not The Godfather Part II, not Raging Bull — Meet the Parents) is one of the best character-comedy moments in the episode. The disqualification ('you can't say Meet the Parents 'cause that's automatically number one') is structurally perfect — he's removing an option by declaring it the winner. High across all craft dimensions.

jokes.text: Selina's museum entrance: 'That's sort of a grey-blue dress. Isn't that something?'
jokes.setup: Selina makes her entrance at the museum gala; the crowd applauds
jokes.punchline: Selina, receiving applause as President, fills the moment by complimenting her own dress in the most underwhelming terms: 'grey-blue,' 'Isn't that something?' — the presidential occasion filled with banal self-appreciation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. 'Grey-blue' is a deliberately weak color description for a gala gown — it sounds like how a man would describe a blue dress. 'Isn't that something?' is classic Selina filler deployed as presidential gravitas.

jokes.text: She's a fucking cunt. You didn't hear it from me.
jokes.setup: A dinner guest is telling a warm story about the Queen of England.
jokes.punchline: Selina, freshly arrived and stressed, not having heard the story, whispers her true opinion of the Queen.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Selina bomb-drop. 'She's a fucking cunt / You didn't hear it from me' is the ideal Veep line: inappropriate, specific, diplomatically catastrophic, and delivered as insider confidence. The 'you didn't hear it from me' addition implies this is a known position she's carefully not shared publicly. Very high quotability.

jokes.text: 'She's a fucking cunt. You didn't hear it from me.'
jokes.setup: Guest mentions they heard a lovely story about the Queen of England
jokes.punchline: Selina's private opinion of the Queen — 'She's a fucking cunt' — delivered as a confidence, followed immediately by 'You didn't hear it from me'
jokes.explanation: Very good. The timing is perfect — the guest has just shared a warm story about the Queen; Selina's response is maximally inappropriate. 'You didn't hear it from me' is an excellent tag — she's sharing state-secret-level personal opinions in a room full of donors. High character integration, high quotability. The 'c-word' is deployed with maximum impact in context.

jokes.text: Karen in court stalling: 'Before we begin, I would love to define a few key terms. Vote. What is a vote? Well, I think that's pretty obvious.' / 'Oh, is it, Your Honor? In many cultures, the word vote can mean a myriad of things. It can mean a celebration. It can mean a dance around a fire. It can mean a silent nod. In this room, what does it mean?'
jokes.setup: Karen is instructed to stall in court while they search for the ballots; she agrees to 'stall' but insists she respects the sanctity of the courtroom
jokes.punchline: Her 'stalling' is to deconstruct the word 'vote' in the most obtuse, culturally relativist way imaginable — 'a dance around a fire' — delivered with total judicial seriousness
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. Karen's lawyerly stalling tactic becomes an absurdist philosophy lecture — 'a dance around a fire' as a definition of 'vote' in an American courtroom is genuinely funny and also a pitch-perfect parody of both legal obfuscation and bad-faith cultural relativism. 'In this room, what does it mean?' is a great closing question that makes the judge complicit. Very strong structure and originality.

jokes.text: What kind of soup do you have today? / Um, what kind of soup is on your mind? / Well, what is today? / Um... it's Thursday. / Thursday? / Yup. / Navy bean. So delicious.
jokes.setup: Selina finds Bob — the disgraced campaign strategist who has clearly had a mental break — apparently at a restaurant or service setting.
jokes.punchline: Bob asks about soup, requiring the day of the week to determine it, confirming complete mental confusion. His final 'Navy bean. So delicious.' is delivered with complete serenity.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed cringe comedy. The soup routine earns its length — each beat ('what kind of soup is on your mind?', needing to know the day, 'Navy bean. So delicious.') adds another layer of gentle tragedy to Bob's decline. The contrast between the stakes (constitutional crisis) and Bob's serene soup inquiry is the entire joke. 'So delicious.' is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Bob at the restaurant soup counter: 'What kind of soup do you have today?' / 'What kind of soup is on your mind?' / 'What is today?' / '...It's Thursday.' / 'Thursday? Navy bean. So delicious.'
jokes.setup: They find Bob at a hotel restaurant ordering soup; he seems calm
jokes.punchline: Bob doesn't know what day it is, works through it with the waiter's help, and arrives at a specific soup preference based purely on the day — 'Thursday? Navy bean.' — his memory system for food is intact while his political memory is gone
jokes.explanation: One of the best moments in the episode — brilliant cringe-comedy writing. The joke has multiple layers: Bob's disorientation (doesn't know the day), the waiter's gentle facilitation ('What kind of soup is on your mind?'), and the punchline that Bob has a completely specific, confident, and correct answer once he has the day. 'Thursday? Navy bean. So delicious.' is perfect — his food memory is intact while his political memory is gone. This is simultaneously the funniest and saddest moment in the episode, and Veep earns both. The economy of the soup exchange is exceptional. Among the top three jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: The postal worker Vance Otlow hoarding ballots was made 'more erratic following post office closures by the Meyer Postal Commission'
jokes.setup: The Justice Department finds 10,000 missing ballots in the home of a postal worker
jokes.punchline: The postal worker was driven erratic by the postal closures from Selina's own Meyer Postal Commission — Selina's modest 'achievement' from the cold open directly caused the ballot crisis she's now benefiting from solving
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most structurally sophisticated joke — a long-form callback that pays off something planted in the first two minutes of the episode. The Meyer Postal Commission's minimal, mocked 'achievement' (reducing debt by 3 billion) directly caused the unstable postal worker who hoarded the ballots that Selina is now counting on to win the presidency. The causality is circular, poetic, and darkly funny: Selina's only real vice-presidential accomplishment is the cause of her presidential crisis and her presidential salvation simultaneously. This is exceptional structural comedy writing — the kind that rewards close attention. Rewatch bonus is guaranteed.

jokes.text: Look at this guy. / Otlow had grown more erratic following post office closures last year by the Meyer Postal Commission.
jokes.setup: A postal worker named Otlow hoarded 10,000 ballots — it's revealed he became erratic after the Meyer Postal Commission's cuts.
jokes.punchline: The man who undermined Selina's election was driven to it by Selina's own postal commission — the same commission Mike cited as her great fiscal achievement earlier.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best structural joke and the payoff of a planted callback from the opening. The Postal Commission — introduced as Selina's pathetic non-achievement — turns out to be the direct cause of the electoral crisis. The circularity is elegant: her marginal budget cut created the man who nearly cost her the presidency. Beautifully constructed episode-level irony.

jokes.text: 'I don't know what you're saying, but you keep doing that math stuff, Kent, no matter how much anyone makes fun of you, okay?'
jokes.setup: Kent delivers another jargon-filled statistical assessment; Selina responds
jokes.punchline: Selina acknowledges she doesn't understand Kent, will never understand Kent, others mock Kent, but Kent should continue — it's a presidential endorsement of incomprehensible expertise delivered as encouragement
jokes.explanation: 'No matter how much anyone makes fun of you' is a great detail — she's offering Kent an official presidential shield against mockery while admitting she has no idea if his work is valid. It's both kind and completely dismissive. Good economy and character integration.

jokes.text: 'I just want you to know that I could not have done it without Richard, and I want you to please relay my thanks to him.' — Selina telling Amy to thank Richard
jokes.setup: Selina tells Amy she's done a great job and couldn't have done it without... Richard
jokes.punchline: The thanks are for Richard, not Amy — delivered to Amy who is asked to be the messenger — a final twist of the knife in the episode's Amy-diminishment arc
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the episode-long Amy diminishment. Amy was told she was 'doing a great job' (a lie to manage her) and now the actual thanks go to Richard via Amy as messenger. 'I'll get right on that' from Amy is perfectly dry. The structural cruelty is earned.

jokes.text: 'Your Honor, how is counsel defining 'deadline' and 'passed'?' — Karen in court
jokes.setup: Opposition objects that the deadline has already passed and the ballots can't be included
jokes.punchline: Karen challenges the definition of 'deadline' and 'passed' — having already redefined 'vote,' she's now going after the other key terms
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to the 'vote' definition stall. Having established her character through the vote monologue, Karen's 'how is counsel defining deadline and passed?' lands as both a running gag and a genuine legal absurdist joke. Economy is near-perfect — twelve words doing massive comedic work as a callback punchline.

jokes.text: After today's stunning turn of events in Nevada, the president has asked Bob Bradley, the Eagle, to return to Washington as her new cybersecurity czar, effective immediately.
jokes.setup: Bob has been revealed as having dementia and has caused multiple disasters.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response is to give Bob a prestigious new title — cybersecurity czar — to move him out of Nevada without explanation.
jokes.explanation: The political tactic of kicking someone upstairs — creating a fake prestigious role to remove a liability — is classic. The fact that the 'cybersecurity czar' role goes to a man who has dementia and was partly responsible for a cybersecurity lie is a nice dark irony.

jokes.text: The sanctions cause China to ban all foreign adoptions by American citizens — 'a bunch of other stuff'
jokes.setup: China responds to Selina's sanctions with its own measures
jokes.punchline: Among the retaliatory measures: 'a ban on all foreign adoptions by American citizens' — a consequence that affects real families, delivered casually, followed by 'a bunch of other stuff'
jokes.explanation: 'A bunch of other stuff' is the key phrase — real human consequences (adoptions blocked) casually dismissed. The joke is that the administration is already treating the blowback from their lie as minor inconvenience. Mike's total lack of concern for the adoption ban is darkly funny.

jokes.text: 'Mike! Mike!' / 'Will the president move forward with her sanctions?' / 'I don't know.' / 'I have to call my wife.'
jokes.setup: Reporters press Mike on whether the president will maintain her China sanctions given the blowback
jokes.punchline: 'I don't know. I have to call my wife.' — Mike's press briefing ends with personal domestic business
jokes.explanation: Excellent closing Mike moment. 'I have to call my wife' in the middle of a press briefing about a diplomatic incident is a great non-sequitur. The implication is that his personal restaurant reservation situation (his wife is at the Salt and Moon table) is more immediately pressing than China policy. Economy is very strong.

jokes.text: Thanks, buttfucker.
jokes.setup: The aide who has escorted Bob to his basement 'office' turns to leave.
jokes.punchline: Bob's sign-off is 'Thanks, buttfucker' — a casually delivered, completely inappropriate profanity as a farewell to a subordinate who just imprisoned him in a basement.
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode-closing line. 'Thanks, buttfucker' works on every level: the incongruity of 'thanks' + the word, the deadpan delivery implying Bob doesn't realize what's happened to him, and the fact that he's thanking the person who just consigned him to a basement prison. The episode ends on this note, which is exactly right. Highest quotability of the episode.

tmdb_overview: Selina sends a tweet and Mike must deal with the consequences. Amy feels usurped by Bob, and begins to be troubled by his behavior. At a museum gala, Gary finds himself the belle of the ball.
insight: Veep's sharpest political satire: 78 jokes in 35 minutes at peak density.
